h1Title: OCD: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (OCD) is a neuropsychiatric condition characterised by recurrent, intrusive obsessions — unwanted thoughts, images, or urges — and compulsions performed to neutralise the distress they provoke. The World Health Organization ranks OCD among the ten leading causes of disability worldwide. It affects approximately 1.1-1.8% of the global population across all ages, with roughly equal prevalence in men and women. Typical onset occurs in late childhood, adolescence, or early adulthood; the disorder runs a chronic fluctuating course in more than 80% of untreated cases. Evidence-based treatment — exposure and response prevention (ERP) therapy, often combined with an SSRI — produces clinically meaningful improvement in 60-70% of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: 1.1-1.8% lifetime prevalence worldwide (Ruscio et al. 2010 National Comorbidity Survey Replication)
keyStats.demographics: Males more commonly affected in childhood (2:1 ratio); sex ratio approaches 1:1 by adulthood; median age of onset is 19-20 years
keyStats.avgAge: Two peaks of onset: ages 10-12 in boys and ages 20-24 across both sexes; symptoms present on average 7-10 years before first treatment
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 70-80 million people affected globally (WHO Global Burden of Disease 2019)

definition: Obsessive-compulsive disorder (ICD-10: F42; DSM-5: 300.3) is characterised by the presence of obsessions, compulsions, or both that are time-consuming (taking more than one hour per day), cause marked distress, and significantly interfere with normal functioning. Obsessions are persistent, intrusive, and unwanted thoughts, images, or impulses that the individual recognises as products of their own mind (ego-dystonic) and attempts to ignore, suppress, or neutralise. Compulsions are repetitive behaviours or mental acts performed in response to an obsession or according to rigid rules, aimed at preventing harm or reducing distress — although the connection to the feared outcome is often excessive or unrealistic. Neurobiologically, OCD involves hyperactivity in orbitofrontal-striatal-thalamic circuits, particularly the caudate nucleus, with excessive cortico-striatal signalling driving repetitive checking and ritualistic behaviour. Serotonin dysregulation is the primary pharmacological target. DSM-5 introduced the obsessive-compulsive and related disorders category, which includes body dysmorphic disorder, hoarding disorder, trichotillomania, and excoriation disorder as related spectrum conditions. OCD is primarily managed by psychiatrists and clinical psychologists specialising in CBT.

typesClassification.description: The most prevalent subtype. Intrusive fears of dirt, germs, bodily fluids, or environmental contaminants drive excessive hand-washing, showering, or surface cleaning that can consume several hours daily and cause skin damage.

Recurring intrusive thoughts about accidentally harming others (leaving a stove on, hitting someone while driving) prompt repetitive checking rituals. The individual's intent is not harmful; these thoughts are ego-dystonic and deeply distressing.
Intolerable feelings of incompleteness or asymmetry drive repetitive arranging, ordering, or touching until an internal 'just right' sense is achieved. Closely related to tic disorders.

typesClassification.type: Forbidden or taboo thoughts (sexual, religious, aggressive)
typesClassification.description: Intrusive sexual, blasphemous, or violent thoughts that are entirely at odds with the person's values. Responses include mental rituals (prayer, reviewing memories), avoidance of triggers, and seeking reassurance. Sometimes called Pure-O, although mental compulsions are always present.

Difficulty discarding possessions due to perceived need and anticipated distress about loss. Now listed as a separate DSM-5 disorder with a distinct treatment pathway but shares orbitofrontal circuit dysregulation with OCD.

primarySymptoms: Recurrent, intrusive, unwanted thoughts that arrive without invitation and are experienced as alien to the individual's values — the hallmark obsession of OCD.
Contamination fears producing repetitive, time-consuming hand-washing or cleaning rituals lasting 30 minutes or more per episode; may cause skin breaks and infection.
Checking compulsions — returning to a door, stove, or switch multiple times despite having visually confirmed it is locked or off, driven by doubt that refuses to resolve.
Mental rituals such as counting, repeating prayers silently, or reviewing memories to neutralise a feared thought — equally disabling as physical compulsions and often invisible to others.
Avoidance behaviour — restructuring daily life to circumvent triggers (avoiding knives, hospitals, churches, pregnant women) to prevent obsession onset; severely restricts activity.
Demand for reassurance from family members or doctors that feared harm has not occurred or will not occur; each reassurance provides brief relief but maintains the cycle.
Intrusive violent, sexual, or blasphemous images that are sharply at odds with the individual's actual character and values, generating profound shame and guilt.
Symmetry and 'just right' compulsions — repeating actions, arranging objects, or touching surfaces until an internal sense of completeness is reached, which may take hours.
Hoarding behaviour — inability to discard ordinary items because of a feared need or emotional attachment, resulting in clutter that impairs living space.
Mental fatigue, irritability, and secondary depression from the sustained cognitive effort of managing obsessions and concealing rituals from others.
earlyWarningSigns: A child who takes significantly longer than peers to complete routine tasks such as washing hands, dressing, or reading — often attributed to perfectionism rather than recognised as OCD.
Requesting excessive reassurance from parents about health, safety, or moral behaviour, with brief relief after each reassurance followed by rapid return of doubt.
Sudden development of rigid, rule-governed bedtime or mealtime routines that provoke intense distress if not followed precisely.
Adolescent reporting intrusive 'bad thoughts' about religion, sexuality, or violence and expressing intense shame — often too embarrassed to disclose the content of the thoughts.
emergencySigns: Active suicidal ideation or a suicide attempt — OCD carries a lifetime suicidal ideation rate of approximately 50% and a completed suicide rate significantly above the general population.
Severe inability to eat, sleep, or maintain hygiene due to contamination fears — urgent inpatient assessment needed if medical deterioration occurs.

New onset of OCD symptoms in a child within 6 weeks of a group-A streptococcal infection — raises the possibility of PANDAS (paediatric autoimmune neuropsychiatric disorder associated with streptococcal infections), requiring urgent paediatric and neurological review.
Acute psychotic break with beliefs that intrusive thoughts are external commands or inserted by outside forces — OCD with poor insight must be distinguished from psychosis urgently.

causes.description: PET and fMRI studies consistently show hyperactivity in the right caudate nucleus, orbitofrontal cortex, and anterior cingulate cortex in OCD. These circuits mediate error-detection and habit-formation; in OCD they generate a persistent 'something is wrong' signal that drives repetitive behaviour despite voluntary effort to stop.

The principal pharmacological evidence for OCD involves serotonin: SSRIs and clomipramine (a TCA with potent serotonin reuptake inhibition) are the only drug classes consistently effective, whereas non-serotonergic drugs are not. Serotonin transporter polymorphisms (5-HTTLPR, 5HT2A) are among the most studied genetic contributors.

OCD heritability is approximately 40-65% from twin studies; higher heritability in early-onset (childhood) cases (65-75%). First-degree relatives of OCD patients have a 4-fold increased risk. Candidate genes include SLC1A1, SAPAP3, and BDNF, though no single gene accounts for substantial variance.

Childhood trauma, abuse, and adverse life events are associated with earlier onset and greater severity in genetically predisposed individuals. Perinatal complications and prenatal stress modestly increase risk. Stressful life events frequently trigger symptom onset or exacerbation.

A subset of childhood-onset OCD follows group-A streptococcal infection (PANDAS) or other infections (PANS); proposed mechanism involves autoantibodies targeting the basal ganglia. Abrupt OCD onset with choreiform movements, tics, and emotional lability after streptococcal infection warrants serological and neurological investigation.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of OCD or OC-spectrum disorders
riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives carry a 4-fold elevated risk; heritability 40-65% in adults and 65-75% in childhood-onset OCD. Co-aggregation with tic disorders (Tourette syndrome) is particularly strong.
Boys have earlier onset and a 2:1 predominance in childhood OCD; the sex ratio equalises by adulthood. Boys are more likely to have tic-related OCD with symmetry and ordering themes.

Physical or sexual abuse, neglect, and chronic bullying are associated with a 2-4-fold increase in OCD onset in genetically vulnerable individuals. Trauma interacts with genetic risk rather than causing OCD independently.

riskFactors.factor: Comorbid tic disorder or Tourette syndrome
riskFactors.description: Approximately 30% of OCD patients have a lifetime tic disorder; tic-related OCD responds less well to SSRIs alone and benefits from antipsychotic augmentation.
Birth complications, maternal infection during the second trimester, and neonatal jaundice have been associated with modestly increased OCD risk in large epidemiological studies.

Abrupt OCD onset within weeks of a documented streptococcal infection in children under 12 may represent an immune-mediated mechanism. Treatment of the infection and immunomodulation can reduce OCD symptoms in confirmed PANDAS cases.

affectedDemographics: Children and adolescents: OCD frequently starts in childhood; approximately one-third of adults with OCD report onset before age 15.
Young adults: the second onset peak at ages 20-24 occurs equally in men and women; stress and life transitions (university, parenthood) are common precipitants.
Postpartum period: perinatal OCD — typically involving intrusive thoughts about harming the baby — affects approximately 1-3% of postpartum women and is often misidentified as postpartum depression.
All socioeconomic and cultural groups: OCD occurs at roughly equal rates across ethnicities and income groups, though access to evidence-based ERP therapy is markedly lower in low-income settings.
diagnosisOverview: OCD diagnosis is clinical, based on structured psychiatric interview using DSM-5 criteria. The Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (Y-BOCS or CY-BOCS for children) is the gold-standard severity measure, assessing time occupied, distress, interference, resistance, and control for both obsessions and compulsions on a 0-40 scale. A score above 16 is generally considered moderate OCD; above 23 indicates severe. Diagnosis requires that obsessions and/or compulsions consume more than one hour per day and cause clinically significant distress or functional impairment. The clinician must rule out: generalised anxiety disorder (worry without ritual), major depression with rumination, schizophrenia with ego-syntonic delusions, body dysmorphic disorder, hoarding disorder, autism spectrum disorder, and tic disorders. The key distinction from OCD-related disorders lies in content: BDD involves solely appearance preoccupation; hoarding involves possessions; trichotillomania involves hair pulling with lesser insight. OCD can present with poor insight or absent insight (formerly called OCD with overvalued ideation), in which the patient partially or fully believes the obsessional fear is realistic — these cases are harder to treat and require the clinician to directly address the belief. NICE NG31 (2005, updated) recommends specialist CBT assessment by a therapist trained in ERP for all newly diagnosed cases.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard clinician-administered severity measure; guides treatment decisions and monitors response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A structured interview of approximately 20-30 minutes assessing 10 items (5 obsession, 5 compulsion subscales) on a 0-4 severity scale. Used at baseline and every 4-8 weeks during treatment. Score above 16 indicates moderate severity requiring active treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes OCD diagnosis per DSM-5 criteria and screens for comorbid disorders including depression, anxiety, tics, and psychosis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 45-90 minute interview covering symptom onset, frequency, distress, and functional impairment. The clinician maps specific obsession and compulsion themes and constructs an initial fear hierarchy for ERP planning.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Patient-completed screening tool identifying obsession-compulsion subtypes and monitoring treatment response
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An 18-42 item self-report questionnaire completed in 5-10 minutes. Not diagnostic alone but guides clinician assessment and ERP hierarchy construction.

diagnosticTests.test: Anti-streptolysin O (ASO) titre and anti-DNase B in children
diagnosticTests.purpose: Supports diagnosis of PANDAS in children with abrupt OCD onset following suspected streptococcal infection
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; results within 2-3 days. An elevated titre confirms recent streptococcal infection but does not alone diagnose PANDAS.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Not routine; used in atypical presentations, rapid late-onset, suspected neurological cause, or suspected PANS/PANDAS with choreiform movements
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-45 minute scan without contrast in most cases. Findings may include caudate hyperintensity in neuroinflammatory cases.

treatmentOverview: The NICE NG31 guideline and the International OCD Foundation (IOCDF) evidence base identify exposure and response prevention (ERP) — a specific form of CBT — as the first-line psychological treatment for OCD at all severity levels. ERP works by systematically exposing the patient to feared stimuli (or thoughts) while resisting compulsive responses, allowing anxiety to habituate and disconfirming catastrophic predictions. Meta-analyses report effect sizes of 1.3-1.5 (large) for ERP versus waitlist; 60-70% of patients achieve clinically significant improvement. NICE recommends ERP as the sole treatment for mild OCD and combined ERP plus SSRI for moderate-to-severe OCD. First-line SSRIs are fluvoxamine, fluoxetine, sertraline, and paroxetine; OCD typically requires higher SSRI doses (often double the antidepressant dose) and a longer trial (at least 12 weeks) before response is assessed. Clomipramine (a tricyclic antidepressant with potent serotonin reuptake inhibition) is equally effective but less well tolerated and is reserved as second-line. For treatment-resistant OCD — defined as inadequate response after two SSRI trials and adequate ERP — augmentation with a low-dose antipsychotic (haloperidol, risperidone, aripiprazole) or D-cycloserine, and referral for deep brain stimulation (DBS), are options in specialist centres. ERP should be delivered by a psychologist or therapist specifically trained in the technique; generic CBT without the exposure component is substantially less effective.

medicalTreatments.name: Sertraline (50-200 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line SSRI for OCD in adults and the most widely prescribed. Requires titration to adequate doses (typically 150-200 mg) and a 10-12 week trial before response is assessed. Better tolerated than clomipramine with a more favourable side-effect profile.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Y-BOCS reduction of 25-35% versus placebo; response (>35% improvement) in 40-60% of patients

medicalTreatments.name: Fluoxetine (40-80 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line SSRI; long half-life reduces risk of discontinuation syndrome. Effective in paediatric OCD (FDA-approved for children ≥7 years). Requires similar high-dose strategy and 12-week trial as other SSRIs.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Y-BOCS reduction 25-35% versus placebo; paediatric trials show comparable benefit to sertraline

medicalTreatments.name: Fluvoxamine (100-300 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: First SSRI formally studied and approved for OCD; evidence base dates to the 1990s. Often used in combination with antipsychotic augmentation in treatment-resistant cases. Divided dosing usually required above 150 mg.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rate approximately 50% in RCTs; equivalent efficacy to other SSRIs in meta-analyses

medicalTreatments.name: Clomipramine (75-250 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Tricyclic antidepressant with the strongest serotonin reuptake inhibition of any antidepressant; considered by some guidelines to be the single most effective pharmacological agent. Restricted by anticholinergic and cardiac side effects. Requires ECG monitoring. Second-line after SSRI failure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Y-BOCS reduction 35-45% in early RCTs; slight numerical superiority to SSRIs in head-to-head trials

medicalTreatments.name: Risperidone augmentation (0.5-3 mg/day)
medicalTreatments.description: Low-dose antipsychotic added to an SSRI in treatment-resistant OCD, particularly when tics co-exist. IOCDF guidelines recommend augmentation with risperidone, haloperidol, or aripiprazole after two failed SSRI trials.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Addition to ongoing SSRI produces a further 10-20% Y-BOCS reduction in approximately 35-40% of refractory cases

medicalTreatments.name: Exposure and response prevention (ERP) — combined with pharmacotherapy
medicalTreatments.description: Structured, graduated exposure to feared stimuli while preventing compulsive responses. Delivered in 13-20 weekly sessions of 60-90 minutes in individual or group format. Combined with an SSRI for moderate-to-severe OCD per NICE NG31.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined ERP+SSRI produces Y-BOCS reduction of 45-55% in RCTs; superior to either alone for moderate-to-severe OCD

surgicalOptions.name: Deep brain stimulation (DBS) of the anterior limb of the internal capsule/nucleus accumbens
surgicalOptions.description: Indicated for severe, treatment-refractory OCD (two adequate SSRI trials, adequate ERP, and continuing severe disability). DBS of the striato-thalamic circuit modulates orbitofrontal hyperactivity. FDA Humanitarian Device Exemption approval since 2009.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Response (>35% Y-BOCS improvement) in approximately 50-60% of carefully selected patients in prospective trials; Mallet et al. 2008 crossover RCT showed significant benefit

surgicalOptions.name: Gamma knife capsulotomy
surgicalOptions.description: Stereotactic radiosurgical ablation of the anterior internal capsule; used when DBS is not feasible. Produces irreversible lesion; offered only at specialist neurosurgical centres after all other treatments have failed.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Sustained response in approximately 45-50% of refractory cases in observational series; delayed effect (months after procedure)

alternativeTreatments.name: Acceptance and Commitment Therapy (ACT) for OCD
alternativeTreatments.description: ACT targets psychological flexibility and values-directed action rather than direct symptom reduction. A 2015 meta-analysis (Bluett et al.) found comparable short-term outcomes to traditional ERP, particularly in patients with poor distress tolerance who struggle with initial ERP exposure.

alternativeTreatments.name: Mindfulness-based interventions as adjunct to ERP
alternativeTreatments.description: Mindfulness training reduces experiential avoidance and improves willingness to enter exposures. A 2018 RCT (Strauss et al.) found mindfulness-integrated CBT superior to a waitlist for OCD symptom severity over 12 weeks.

whySeeSpecialist: A psychiatrist or clinical psychologist specifically trained in OCD is essential for treatment planning, particularly for SSRI dose titration to levels above the standard antidepressant range and for delivering or supervising ERP. Generic mental health treatment without OCD-specific ERP and adequate pharmacotherapy is associated with poor long-term outcomes.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a psychiatrist or clinical psychologist with at least 2 years' experience in OCD specifically, and who uses the Y-BOCS to monitor treatment. Verify that the therapist delivers structured ERP, not general supportive therapy. The International OCD Foundation (iocdf.org) provides a therapist directory.
hospitalCriteria: Severe OCD with functional collapse (inability to eat, maintain hygiene, leave the house)
Active suicidality requiring inpatient safety monitoring
Paediatric PANDAS with acute neuropsychiatric symptoms requiring intravenous immunoglobulin or plasmapheresis
Post-DBS or gamma knife complication requiring neurosurgical assessment
Inpatient intensive ERP programme for treatment-resistant cases
keyFacilities: Specialist OCD clinics within psychiatry departments
Clinical psychology services with CBT/ERP trained therapists
Neurosurgical centres offering DBS (tertiary level only)
Child and adolescent mental health services (CAMHS) for paediatric OCD
preventionStrategies: Early recognition and treatment during childhood onset reduces the risk of chronic disabling illness; parents should seek assessment for any child whose daily routines are dominated by rituals lasting more than 30 minutes.

Avoid accommodating compulsions within the family home — family accommodation (performing rituals for the patient, providing reassurance) maintains OCD and should be systematically reduced as part of treatment.
Address childhood adversity and trauma exposure through evidence-based psychotherapy early; this reduces the threshold for OCD onset in genetically predisposed individuals.
Treat streptococcal throat infections promptly in children with known risk factors for PANDAS to reduce the inflammatory trigger.
Psychoeducation about the nature of intrusive thoughts for at-risk young people reduces shame, improves help-seeking, and enables earlier intervention.
lifestyleModifications: Practise response prevention daily — resist completing compulsions for as long as possible, starting with the least distressing triggers, to accumulate tolerance to obsessional distress.
Reduce family accommodation systematically under the guidance of a therapist — each reassurance episode reinforces OCD and delays recovery.
Maintain regular sleep, exercise, and nutrition — sleep deprivation and poor physical health both worsen OCD symptom severity.
Aerobic exercise 3-5 times per week at moderate intensity is associated with a modest reduction in OCD severity (effect size d=0.4) in meta-analyses.
Keep a symptom diary logging daily Y-BOCS estimate, hours spent on rituals, and stressors — identifies triggers and demonstrates improvement that is not always subjectively apparent.
Engage actively with peer support groups (OCDUK, IOCDF network) — shared experience reduces shame and normalises the experience of intrusive thoughts.

recommended: Regular balanced meals at consistent times to maintain stable energy and mood, which reduces vulnerability to OCD symptom spikes
Omega-3 fatty acid rich foods (oily fish, walnuts) — adjunctive benefit suggested in anxiety and OCD-spectrum conditions
Magnesium-rich foods (nuts, seeds, dark leafy greens) — low magnesium is associated with heightened anxiety and arousal
Adequate protein throughout the day to maintain tryptophan availability for serotonin synthesis
avoid: Caffeine above moderate amounts (>200 mg/day) — increases arousal and anxiety that exacerbate OCD symptom severity
Alcohol — short-term anxiolytic effect is followed by rebound anxiety and worsened OCD; interacts poorly with SSRIs
High-sugar, ultra-processed foods causing blood glucose instability that heightens irritability and anxiety
Irregular or skipped meals, which destabilise mood and lower the threshold for obsessional distress

exerciseGuidelines: Aerobic exercise is recommended as an adjunct to standard OCD treatment. Three to five sessions per week of 30-40 minutes of moderate aerobic activity (running, cycling, swimming) reduce anxiety and OCD symptom severity. Group exercise is particularly beneficial by providing social engagement and structured activity that interrupts ritualistic behaviour patterns.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed SSRI at the same time each day without skipping doses — consistency is critical for maintaining the required high serotonin levels for OCD response.
Practise at least one planned ERP exercise daily between therapy sessions, starting with the lowest-anxiety item on the fear hierarchy.
Log daily compulsion time in a brief diary — visible progress motivates continued treatment adherence and provides data for the therapist.
Communicate actively with family members about reducing accommodation; provide them with simple scripts from the therapist for refusing requests for reassurance.
Attend scheduled therapy appointments reliably; OCD responds less well to sporadic or low-frequency ERP sessions.
Contact the treating team promptly if thoughts become suicidal or self-harm occurs — OCD carries elevated suicide risk, particularly during periods of symptom escalation.
prognosis: OCD runs a chronic course in most individuals, but outcome depends strongly on treatment adequacy. With combined ERP and SSRI, approximately 60-70% of patients achieve clinically significant improvement (Y-BOCS reduction >35%), and 25-35% achieve remission (Y-BOCS score below 8). Without evidence-based treatment, fewer than 20% show meaningful spontaneous improvement over 10 years (Skoog & Skoog 1999, 40-year follow-up). Predictors of poorer outcome include early onset, poor insight, hoarding subtype, comorbid schizotypal personality disorder, and non-adherence to ERP. Suicide risk is substantial: lifetime suicidal ideation in approximately 50%, and completed suicide rates estimated at 0.3-1% in population-based studies. Continuous pharmacotherapy is recommended for at least 1-2 years after remission; abrupt SSRI discontinuation is associated with a 90% relapse rate within one year in OCD (compared with ~50% in depression).
recoveryTimeline: SSRIs require 10-12 weeks to produce a meaningful reduction in Y-BOCS scores in OCD; full response may take 3-6 months at adequate doses. ERP therapy typically requires 13-20 sessions over 3-5 months for moderate OCD. Combined treatment shows response by 3-6 months, with continued improvement over 12-24 months. Dose reduction and eventual discontinuation can be considered after 1-2 years of remission under specialist guidance.
complications: Chronic occupational disability — severe OCD frequently prevents full-time employment; quality of life measures in untreated OCD rank comparable to severe depression and schizophrenia.
Secondary major depression — 67% of people with OCD develop depression at some point; depression worsens OCD and significantly increases suicide risk.
Social isolation and relationship breakdown — rituals, avoidance, and family accommodation progressively restrict social functioning and strain intimate relationships.
Physical complications from rituals — hand-washing dermatitis and skin breakdown, tooth and enamel erosion from excessive brushing, and musculoskeletal strain from repetitive movements.
Harmful delay to diagnosis — the average person with OCD waits 7-10 years before receiving the correct diagnosis and evidence-based treatment; chronic entrenchment worsens prognosis.
SSRI discontinuation relapse — abrupt cessation of pharmacotherapy is associated with 90% relapse within one year; managed taper under psychiatric supervision is mandatory.

supportResources.name: OCD-UK
supportResources.description: UK charity providing helpline, peer forums, therapist directories, and self-help resources specifically for OCD.

supportResources.name: International OCD Foundation (IOCDF)
supportResources.description: US-based foundation providing therapist directories, treatment guidelines, annual conferences, and detailed patient education on ERP.

supportResources.name: NICE NG31: Obsessive-compulsive disorder and body dysmorphic disorder
supportResources.description: UK clinical guideline on stepped-care treatment of OCD and BDD, freely accessible to patients and clinicians.

supportResources.name: Beyond OCD — patient resources
supportResources.description: Free evidence-based information about OCD, ERP, finding specialists, and family guidance maintained by the Beyond OCD Foundation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Generalised Anxiety Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: GAD involves persistent, wide-ranging worry about realistic life concerns (health, finances, relationships) without compulsive rituals. OCD obsessions are typically ego-dystonic, restricted to specific feared themes, and accompanied by compulsive rituals aimed at neutralisation. GAD responds well to SSRIs at standard doses and non-ERP CBT; OCD requires higher SSRI doses and ERP specifically.

confusedWithConditions.name: Major Depressive Disorder
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Depression causes ruminative, often negative-self-focused thinking that can resemble obsessions, but lacks the ego-dystonic quality and compulsive response. In OCD the content of obsessions is feared rather than believed, and rituals are driven by anxiety reduction rather than mood. Both conditions frequently co-occur.

confusedWithConditions.name: ADHD
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: ADHD produces difficulty suppressing irrelevant thoughts and impulsive behaviour, but these are not experienced as intrusive and ego-dystonic as OCD obsessions. ADHD patients typically do not perform compulsive rituals. Both ADHD and OCD involve orbitofrontal-striatal dysregulation and occasionally co-occur.

confusedWithConditions.name: Schizophrenia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: OCD with poor or absent insight (the patient fully believes the obsessional fear) can resemble psychosis, but the content of OCD beliefs concerns harm prevention, contamination, or symmetry — not ego-syntonic delusions or external control. Thought insertion in schizophrenia involves the experience that thoughts are implanted from outside; OCD patients recognise the obsession as their own.

relatedConditions.name: PTSD
relatedConditions.relevance: Both OCD and PTSD involve intrusive mental content driving avoidance and safety behaviour; differentiating trauma-related obsessions from PTSD intrusions guides treatment selection.

relatedConditions.name: ADHD
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares orbitofrontal-striatal dysregulation; the two conditions can co-occur and complicate pharmacological management.

relatedConditions.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder
relatedConditions.relevance: Repetitive behaviours in autism can resemble OCD compulsions but are typically ego-syntonic; the two conditions co-occur and require combined management strategies.

relatedConditions.name: Schizophrenia
relatedConditions.relevance: OCD occurs in up to 25% of people with schizophrenia; clozapine can paradoxically worsen OCD symptoms.

faqs.question: What is OCD in simple terms?
faqs.answer: OCD is a condition where unwanted, repetitive thoughts (obsessions) cause intense anxiety, and repetitive actions (compulsions) are performed to relieve that anxiety temporarily. The relief is short-lived, keeping the person trapped in a cycle. OCD is not about being tidy or careful — it is a disabling neuropsychiatric disorder.

faqs.question: What causes OCD?
faqs.answer: OCD results from dysregulation of orbitofrontal-striatal-thalamic circuits that govern error-detection and habit formation, with serotonin system involvement. Genetic factors account for 40-65% of risk. Environmental triggers include childhood adversity, stress, and in children, post-streptococcal infection in susceptible individuals.

faqs.question: What is the best treatment for OCD?
faqs.answer: Exposure and response prevention (ERP) therapy is the first-line treatment for OCD at all severity levels. Combined with an SSRI (sertraline, fluoxetine, or fluvoxamine) for moderate-to-severe OCD, it produces response in 60-70% of patients. Higher SSRI doses than used for depression are typically required.

faqs.question: Can OCD be cured permanently?
faqs.answer: OCD is rarely permanently cured but is highly treatable. Many patients achieve remission (Y-BOCS score below 8) with combined ERP and SSRI therapy. Relapse after stopping medication is common (90% within one year), so long-term maintenance treatment is usually recommended.

faqs.question: Is OCD an anxiety disorder?
faqs.answer: OCD was historically classified as an anxiety disorder but DSM-5 (2013) moved it to its own category: obsessive-compulsive and related disorders. Anxiety is central to OCD, but its unique features — ego-dystonic intrusive thoughts and compulsive rituals — distinguish it from generalised anxiety or phobia.

faqs.question: How is OCD diagnosed?
faqs.answer: OCD is diagnosed clinically using DSM-5 criteria and the Yale-Brown Obsessive Compulsive Scale (Y-BOCS). Symptoms must consume more than one hour per day and cause significant distress or functional impairment. There is no blood test or brain scan that confirms OCD; diagnosis requires a specialist psychiatric interview.

faqs.question: What are common OCD themes or subtypes?
faqs.answer: Common OCD themes include contamination and washing, harm and checking, symmetry and ordering, and forbidden (sexual, violent, or religious) thoughts. Each theme involves specific obsessions and compulsions. Treatment targets the underlying OCD cycle regardless of specific theme.

faqs.question: Does OCD get worse with age?
faqs.answer: Without treatment, OCD typically worsens over time as avoidance expands and compulsions grow more elaborate. With adequate ERP and medication, most patients stabilise or improve. Life stressors, hormonal changes, and stopping medication can cause relapses at any age.

faqs.question: Is OCD genetic?
faqs.answer: OCD has a heritability of approximately 40-65% from twin studies, rising to 65-75% in childhood-onset cases. First-degree relatives of OCD patients carry a 4-fold increased risk. No single gene causes OCD; multiple genes of small effect interact with environmental factors.

faqs.question: Can children have OCD?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately one-third of adults with OCD had onset before age 15. Childhood OCD presents with similar obsession themes to adult OCD but often involves family accommodation. ERP adapted for children and family involvement in treatment is first-line; SSRIs are added for moderate-to-severe cases.

faqs.question: What is exposure and response prevention (ERP)?
faqs.answer: ERP is a structured CBT technique where the patient is gradually exposed to feared stimuli or thoughts and refrains from performing compulsive rituals, allowing anxiety to habituate naturally. It is the most effective psychological treatment for OCD with effect sizes of 1.3-1.5 versus waitlist in meta-analyses.

faqs.question: How is OCD different from everyday worrying?
faqs.answer: OCD obsessions are ego-dystonic: they feel alien, horrifying, and inconsistent with the person's values. Everyday worries are about realistic concerns and are ego-syntonic. OCD is accompanied by compulsive rituals or avoidance that significantly disrupts daily function, whereas ordinary worry is not.

faqs.question: What medications treat OCD?
faqs.answer: SSRIs (sertraline, fluoxetine, fluvoxamine, paroxetine) and clomipramine are the evidence-based pharmacological treatments for OCD. OCD requires higher SSRI doses and longer trials (10-12 weeks minimum) than depression. For treatment-resistant cases, low-dose antipsychotic augmentation (risperidone, aripiprazole) is added.

faqs.question: Does OCD increase the risk of suicide?
faqs.answer: Yes. Approximately 50% of people with OCD experience suicidal ideation at some point. Suicide risk is highest when OCD is severe, when depression co-occurs, and when the person experiences profound shame about their obsessional content. Urgent psychiatric review is needed for any suicidal ideation.

faqs.question: What is Pure-O OCD?
faqs.answer: Pure-O is an informal term for OCD dominated by intrusive thoughts (sexual, violent, or religious) with no visible compulsions. Mental rituals — silent counting, reviewing, praying, or reassurance-seeking — are always present but hidden. Pure-O responds to ERP targeting mental compulsions as well as overt ones.

metaDescription: OCD: obsessive-compulsive disorder causing intrusive thoughts and repetitive rituals in 1.1-1.8% of people. ERP therapy and SSRI treatment explained.
